text:
Over the years, the new immigration policy favors the younger age groups for long-term economic gains. The only exceptions have been the parental and grand-parental reunification programs aiming to solve the spatial issue between the immigrant and his family. For nearly 150 years, the immigration of seniors has been an undisputed law. In fact, seniors constitute, without a doubt, the least nuisance immigrant group, as they present almost no criminal record, are less involved in the complex and numerous links of the so-called chain migration, and therefore pose no problem regarding the preservation of the identity of the host country.

Although the right to reunification with elderly family members in their homelands is still in place, over the last few decades there has been a clandestine, albeit relentless retrenchment of this right under the guise of economic necessity. In this way, the receiving countries, under various and changing justifications, have reduced the size of the annual migration quotas, established a number of barriers to access to necessary financial sponsors for the applicant, implemented health examinations to determine if the immigrant has any illness that might be a burden on the pension system, and further diminished the extent of families allowed to join their loved ones. This paper examines the restrictions on elderly family unification in the three mentioned cases of settler states, new settler states and liberal states trying to restrict free immigration and demonstrates that the legal, political and economic justification for them are ungrounded and therefore their limitation to reunification with elderly family members is not only unjustifiable but also irrational. Thus, there should be a reversal of these limitations.
